verses.text: The serpent's first move is a question that reframes God's generous gift as a restriction. God said every tree is freely yours; the serpent asks whether God restricted every tree. The Hebrew word for crafty even echoes 'naked' from the previous verse.
The woman answers — and adds something God never said. He said don't eat. She adds don't even touch. Small additions to God's word still distort it.
The woman answers — and adds something God never said. He said don't eat. She adds don't even touch. Small additions to God's word still distort it.

The serpent flatly contradicts God and twists His motive: He is holding back from you, He doesn't want you to grow. The half-truth is the most dangerous part — eyes will open, but not the way the serpent promises.

The serpent flatly contradicts God and twists His motive: He is holding back from you, He doesn't want you to grow. The half-truth is the most dangerous part — eyes will open, but not the way the serpent promises.

Three pulls work on her at once: good for food, beautiful to look at, desirable to make her wise. She eats. And notice — her husband was with her. He watched silently and then ate too. The fall is shared, not hers alone.
Their eyes do open — the serpent didn't lie about that. But what they see is shame, not glory. Their first instinct is to cover, to hide who they are. Sin teaches us to fear being seen.
God comes walking in the garden as He apparently always did — this was normal intimacy. But for the first time the humans hide. Sin's first effect is not punishment, but distance.
God knows where Adam is. The question is for Adam, not for God. Where are you? is the first question in the Bible, and it has been God's question to runaway hearts ever since.
Adam admits he hid because he was afraid and naked. But naked is not the real problem; Adam was naked yesterday too. The real problem is the new awareness — fear has entered his world.
God's second question gives Adam the chance to come clean. Notice God doesn't accuse; He asks. He gives the man room to confess.
Adam blames the woman whom you gave to be with me — passing the blame to Eve and to God in one breath. Eve passes it to the serpent. Sin's other instinct, learned the same day: never my fault.
Adam blames the woman whom you gave to be with me — passing the blame to Eve and to God in one breath. Eve passes it to the serpent. Sin's other instinct, learned the same day: never my fault.
God's first curse falls on the serpent. He is brought low, made to eat dust — a posture of permanent defeat.

Inside the curse is the first promise of the gospel. One day, a descendant of the woman will crush the serpent's head — a wound that ends the war. The serpent will only wound a heel — painful, but not fatal.

To the woman, God names what sin has broken in her: pain in motherhood, and tension with her husband. He will rule over you describes a consequence of the fall, not God's original design. Scripture is naming a wound, not creating one.
To the man, God names the new burden: the ground will resist him. Thorns and thistles will grow alongside crops. Work, once pure cultivation, now includes frustration.
To the man, God names the new burden: the ground will resist him. Thorns and thistles will grow alongside crops. Work, once pure cultivation, now includes frustration.
From dust you came, to dust you return. Death enters with these words — quietly, factually. The body God shaped in chapter 2 will one day go back to the ground that gave it.
Right after the curse, Adam names his wife Eve — meaning mother of all living. In the middle of judgment, he is reaching for hope. Life will continue.
God Himself clothes them — not with fig leaves of their own making, but with coats of skin. An animal died for these coverings. The first sacrifice happens here, gently, on their behalf.
God notes the new danger: if they reach for the tree of life now, in this broken state, they will live forever wounded. Exile is a mercy, not only a punishment.
God sends them out of Eden, with cherubim and a flaming sword guarding the way back. They are not abandoned — but the door behind them is closed until something else opens it.
God sends them out of Eden, with cherubim and a flaming sword guarding the way back. They are not abandoned — but the door behind them is closed until something else opens it.
